title: When feeling a pleasant feeling, he understands: ‘It is impermanent’; he understands: ‘It is not to be clung to’; he understands: ‘It is not to…
content: When feeling a pleasant feeling, he understands: ‘It is impermanent’; he understands: ‘It is not to be clung to’; he understands: ‘It is not to be delighted in.’ When feeling a painful feeling, he understands: ‘It is impermanent’; he understands: ‘It is not to be clung to’; he understands: ‘It is not to be delighted in.’ When feeling a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, he understands: ‘It is impermanent’; he understands: ‘It is not to be clung to’; he understands: ‘It is not to be delighted in.’ If he feels a pleasant feeling, he feels it as one who is not bound. If he feels a painful feeling … If he feels a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, he feels it as one who is not bound. When he feels a feeling ending with the body, he understands: ‘I feel a feeling ending with the body.’ When he feels a feeling ending with life, he understands: ‘I feel a feeling ending with life.’ He understands: ‘With the breakup of the body, after death, everything that is felt, in which he no longer delights, will become cool right here.’ Just as, Assaji, an oil lamp burns in dependence on oil and a wick. When the oil and wick of that lamp are exhausted, with no further fuel, the lamp is extinguished. So too, Assaji, a monk, when feeling a feeling ending with the body, understands … when feeling a feeling ending with life, understands … after death, everything that is felt, in which he no longer delights, becomes cool.”

title: “Furthermore, Cunda, here a person is infatuated, addicted, living surrounded by the five strands of sensual pleasure; that is the fourth kind of pleasure. Cunda,…
content: “Furthermore, Cunda, here a person is infatuated, addicted, living surrounded by the five strands of sensual pleasure; that is the fourth kind of pleasure. Cunda, these four kinds of pleasure are low, vulgar, worldly, ignoble, unbeneficial, not leading to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, and Nibbāna. Cunda, this is possible: wanderers of other sects might say: ‘The Sakyan ascetics are living addicted to these four kinds of pleasure.’ They should be answered: ‘That is not so; you speak wrongly. You misrepresent us, untruthfully, falsely.’”

title: - “Venerable Sāriputta, what is happiness? What is suffering?” - “Friend, rebirth is suffering; non-rebirth is happiness. When there is rebirth, friend, this suffering is…
content:
- “Venerable Sāriputta, what is happiness? What is suffering?”
- “Friend, rebirth is suffering; non-rebirth is happiness. When there is rebirth, friend, this suffering is to be expected: cold, heat, hunger, thirst, defecation, urination, contact with fire, contact with a stick, contact with a sword. Even relatives and friends, when they meet and gather together, cause distress. Friend, rebirth is suffering; when there is rebirth, this suffering is to be expected. When there is no rebirth, friend, this happiness is to be expected: no cold, no heat, no hunger, no thirst, no defecation, no urination, no contact with fire, no contact with a stick, no contact with a sword. Even relatives and friends, when they meet and gather together, do not cause distress. Friend, when there is no rebirth, this happiness is to be expected.”

title: “Rāhula, develop the meditation on foulness. Rāhula, by developing the meditation on foulness, what is called lust is abandoned. Rāhula, develop the meditation on impermanence.…
content: “Rāhula, develop the meditation on foulness. Rāhula, by developing the meditation on foulness, what is called lust is abandoned. Rāhula, develop the meditation on impermanence. Rāhula, by developing the meditation on impermanence, what is called the conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned.”

title: “Monks, just as a forest deer, walking in the forest, walks confidently, stands confidently, sits confidently, lies down confidently. Why is that? Because it has…
content: “Monks, just as a forest deer, walking in the forest, walks confidently, stands confidently, sits confidently, lies down confidently. Why is that? Because it has escaped the hunter’s reach. So too, monks, a monk, secluding himself from sensual desires, secluding himself from unwholesome states, enters and dwells in the first jhāna, which is accompanied by initial and sustained thought, with rapture and pleasure born of seclusion. Monks, that monk is called one who has blinded Māra, who has destroyed his track, who has escaped the sight of Māra.”

title: “Monks, form is impermanent. What is impermanent is suffering. What is suffering is not-self. What is not-self should be seen as it really is with…
content: “Monks, form is impermanent. What is impermanent is suffering. What is suffering is not-self. What is not-self should be seen as it really is with correct wisdom: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.’ Seeing thus with correct wisdom as it actually is, the mind becomes dispassionate and liberated, not grasping the defilements.”

title: “Here, Udāyi, a person lives aiming at the abandoning of attachments, the relinquishment of attachments. But while he lives aiming at the abandoning of attachments,…
content: “Here, Udāyi, a person lives aiming at the abandoning of attachments, the relinquishment of attachments. But while he lives aiming at the abandoning of attachments, the relinquishment of attachments, sometimes, from time to time, distorted thoughts, thoughts and considerations connected with attachments continue to arise. Udāyi, mindfulness arises slowly and he quickly abandons that thought, dispels it, ends it, destroys it. Udāyi, just as a man might drop two or three drops of water onto an iron pan that has been heated all day—slowly those drops fall, quickly they evaporate and vanish. Udāyi, I say: ‘This person is still bound, not freed.’ Why is that? Udāyi, the difference in his faculties has been fully understood by me.”

title: “And how, householder, is wandering without a home? Regarding the form element, householder, whatever there is of desire, lust, delight, craving, any engagement and clinging,…
content: “And how, householder, is wandering without a home? Regarding the form element, householder, whatever there is of desire, lust, delight, craving, any engagement and clinging, mental standpoints, attachments, underlying tendencies — the Tathāgata has abandoned them, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, made them non-existent, so that they cannot arise again in the future. Therefore, the Tathāgata is called ‘one who wanders without a home.’ Regarding the feeling element … perception element … formation element … consciousness element — the Tathāgata has abandoned them … Therefore, the Tathāgata is called ‘one who wanders without a home.’”

title: “And, monks, when that monk dwells in these five things, four more things should be developed. The perception of foulness should be developed to abandon…
content: “And, monks, when that monk dwells in these five things, four more things should be developed. The perception of foulness should be developed to abandon greed; loving-kindness should be developed to abandon ill-will; mindfulness of breathing should be developed to cut off thoughts; the perception of impermanence should be developed to uproot the conceit ‘I am.’ For a monk with the perception of impermanence, monks, the perception of not-self becomes established. For one with the perception of not-self, the conceit ‘I am’ is uprooted, and Nibbāna is attained in this very life.”

title: Stop, thoughts of sensual desire! Stop, thoughts of ill-will! Stop, thoughts of harmfulness! Enough, thoughts of sensual desire! Enough, thoughts of ill-will! Enough, thoughts of…
content:
Stop, thoughts of sensual desire!
Stop, thoughts of ill-will!
Stop, thoughts of harmfulness!
Enough, thoughts of sensual desire!
Enough, thoughts of ill-will!
Enough, thoughts of harmfulness!
